title: Hávamál
draftNote: Gestaþáttr, the opening gnomic section (stanzas 1–79). Being prepared and published in batches from Neckel 1914 — stanzas 1–20 are done so far; the rest are on the way.
source: Gustav Neckel, Edda: Die Lieder des Codex Regius nebst verwandten Denkmälern, 1st edition (Heidelberg: Carl Winter, 1914), Hávamál, stanzas 1–79 (the Gestaþáttr), pp. 16–29. Text transcribed from Neckel's page images and reproduced in his normalization exactly — þ, ð, æ, œ, the nasal vowel ǫ, ø, acute length-marks, the -z/-az/-oz middle endings, i for j (sitia, miǫk), -om datives, þvíat as one word. Italicized (editorially emended or expanded) letters are set as plain letters; major emendations are noted. Eddic poems are cited by stanza, so the margin numbers are stanza numbers. Pronunciation convention: reconstructed classical Old Icelandic, c. 1200. Translations made fresh from the original.
about: The Hávamál (‘Sayings of the High One’) survives in a single medieval manuscript, the Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to, c. 1270); everything printed here is a modern editor's normalization of that one source, and the standard spelling itself is a scholarly convention, not something the scribe wrote consistently. The Gestaþáttr — ‘the guest's section’ — is the first and most famous part: loosely linked proverbs of hospitality, caution, drink, friendship, and conduct, voiced as if by Odin. The verse is ljóðaháttr, the chant-meter of Norse wisdom poetry. The numbers in the margin are stanza numbers, the usual way of citing Eddic verse.

sentences.t:
Gáttir allar,
áðr gangi fram,
um skoðaz skyli,
um skygnaz skyli;
þvíat óvíst er at vita,
hvar óvinir sitia á fleti fyrir.
sentences.l:
All the doorways,
before one walks forward,
should be scanned,
should be spied out;
for it is uncertain to know
where enemies sit on the hall-floor ahead.
sentences.i: Before you step through any doorway, look it all over, scout it well — for you can never know for certain where enemies may be sitting in the hall ahead of you.
sentences.n: The poem opens in ljóðaháttr, the ‘chant meter’ of Old Norse wisdom verse: paired short lines whose stressed syllables alliterate (Gáttir–gangi; skoðaz–skygnaz). skyli ‘should’ is the gnomic subjunctive of skulu — it gives proverbs their timeless ‘one should…’. skoðaz / skygnaz end in the -z middle voice (worn down from -sk ‘oneself’), roughly ‘be scanned / peer about’. um before a verb is an old, now-empty particle — ignore it in the sense. flet (dat. fleti) is the raised plank-floor along a hall's walls where people sat. Neckel prints this text in an older normalized spelling (þvíat one word, sitia for sitja, -z endings); we follow his 1914 edition exactly.

sentences.t:
Gefendr heilir!
Gestr er inn kominn:
hvar skal sitia siá?
miǫk er bráðr,
sá er at brǫndom skal
síns um freista frama.
sentences.l:
Givers, hail!
A guest is come in:
where shall this one sit?
Very hasty is he,
who at the fire-logs must
make trial of his own worth.
sentences.i: Good health to the givers! A guest has stepped inside — where is he to sit? The man who must prove his worth at the hearth-fire stands there in an anxious hurry.
sentences.n: A greeting to the hosts: Gefendr ‘the givers’ (of hospitality), heilir ‘hale, whole’ — the ancestor of English hail and wassail. inn is the adverb ‘in’ (gestr er inn kominn, ‘a guest has come in’); siá is the demonstrative ‘this one’ (nom.), not to be confused with sá ‘that one, he’. The letter ǫ in miǫk and brǫndom is Neckel's spelling of a nasal o-vowel (later Icelandic ö); use the ‘þ→th’ button to fold it to a plain o. The second half is debated: at brǫndom is literally ‘at the fire-logs’ (the hearth), and síns um freista frama ‘to make trial of his own worth’ — we keep the literal image and flag the doubt.

sentences.t:
Elz er þǫrf,
þeims inn er kominn
ok á kné kalinn;
matar ok váða
er manni þǫrf,
þeim er hefir um fiall farit.
sentences.l:
Of fire there is need,
for him who is come in
and is chilled to the knee;
of food and clothing
has a man need,
he who has fared over the fell.
sentences.i: Fire is what a man needs when he comes in numb with cold to the knee; food and dry clothes he needs, after crossing the mountains.
sentences.n: Elz = eld(s), genitive of eldr ‘fire’, governed by þǫrf ‘need’ (er þǫrf + genitive = ‘there is need of’). þeims = þeim es, ‘for him who’. um fiall farit: to have travelled across the highland — cold, wet, and hungry on arrival.

sentences.t:
Vatz er þǫrf,
þeim er til verðar kømr,
þerro ok þióðlaðar,
góðs um œðis,
ef sér geta mætti,
orz ok endrþǫgo.
sentences.i: A guest coming to a meal needs water, a towel, and a friendly welcome — a kind manner, if he can find it, a fair hearing and, in his turn, a courteous silence.
sentences.n: A quietly interpretive stanza. þióðlaðar (gen. of þióðlǫð) = a hearty invitation/welcome. góðs um œðis: ‘of good disposition’ (œði ‘temper, manner’). orz ok endrþǫgo: ‘of speech and of silence-in-return’ — the guest should be both heard and, when it is his turn to listen, silent.

sentences.i: A man who travels far needs his wits about him; at home anything passes, but the man who knows nothing and sits among the clever becomes a laughing-stock.
sentences.n: dælt (from dæll) ‘easy, undemanding’: heima hvat ‘anything at home’ — among your own, ignorance is not exposed. at augabragði verðr = ‘becomes (an object) of a wink/mockery’, i.e. a laughing-stock. með snotrom ‘among the wise’ (dat. pl. of snotr).

sentences.t:
At hyggiandi sinni
skylit maðr hrœsinn vera,
heldr gætinn at geði;
þá er horskr ok þǫgull
kømr heimisgarða til,
sialdan verðr víti vǫrom;
þvíat óbrigðra vin
fær maðr aldregi
en manvit mikit.
sentences.l:
Of his understanding
a man should not be boastful,
but rather wary in mind;
when a wise and silent man
comes to a homestead,
harm seldom befalls the wary;
for a more faithful friend
a man never gets
than much good sense.
sentences.i: A man should not flaunt his cleverness, but keep it guarded; when a shrewd, quiet man comes to a house, misfortune rarely touches the careful — for no friend is more dependable than a good store of common sense.
sentences.n: An expanded stanza (nine lines). skylit = skyli-at, ‘should not’. hrœsinn ‘boastful’; gætinn at geði ‘guarded in mind’. vǫrom (dat. pl. of varr ‘wary’). The closing couplet is proverbial: manvit ‘mother-wit, common sense’ is the truest friend.

sentences.t:
Inn vari gestr,
er til verðar kømr,
þunno hlióði þegir,
eyrom hlýðir,
en augom skoðar;
svá nýsiz fróðra hverr fyrir.
sentences.l:
The wary guest,
who comes to a meal,
keeps silent with taut hearing,
listens with his ears,
and looks about with his eyes;
so every wise man scouts ahead.
sentences.i: A cautious guest at a meal keeps quiet, listening keenly and watching closely — that is how every prudent man feels his way.
sentences.n: þunno hlióði þegir: ‘is silent with thin/taut hearing’ — alert, straining to catch everything. nýsiz … fyrir ‘scouts ahead, feels the ground’ (middle voice of nýsa). Echoes the caution of stanza 1.

sentences.t:
Hinn er sæll,
er sér um getr
lof ok líknstafi;
ódælla er við þat,
er maðr eiga skal
annars brióstom í.
sentences.l:
He is fortunate,
who gets for himself
praise and words of favour;
far harder it is
with what a man must own
inside another's breast.
sentences.i: Fortunate is the man who wins praise and goodwill by his own doing; it is a chancier thing to depend on what lives inside someone else's heart.
sentences.n: líknstafi (acc. pl. of líknstafr) ‘words/tokens of favour, kindly regard’. ódælla ‘less easy, harder’ (comparative of ódæll). The point: esteem you earn yourself is secure; what you must keep in another's breast (their goodwill) is not in your control.

sentences.t:
Sá er sæll,
er siálfr um á
lof ok vit, meðan lifir;
þvíat ill ráð
hefir maðr opt þegit
annars brióstom ór.
sentences.l:
He is fortunate,
who for himself has
praise and wit, while he lives;
for ill counsel
a man has often received
out of another's breast.
sentences.i: Happy the man who owns praise and good sense of his own while he lives — for many a man has taken bad advice out of someone else's heart.
sentences.n: A companion to stanza 8: better to carry your own judgement (vit) than to draw counsel from another's breast, which is often ill (ill ráð).

sentences.t:
Byrði betri
berrat maðr brauto at,
en sé manvit mikit;
auði betra
þikkir þat í ókunnom stað,
slíkt er válaðs vera.
sentences.l:
A better burden
a man bears not on the road
than much good sense;
better than riches
it seems in an unknown place,
such is the refuge of the destitute.
sentences.i: No better load can a man carry on his travels than plenty of common sense; in a strange place it is worth more than wealth — it is the poor man's refuge.
sentences.n: berrat = berr-at ‘bears not’. brauto at (dat. of braut ‘road’) ‘on the way’. auði betra ‘better than wealth’ (dat. of auðr). válaðs vera: ‘the being/refuge of the destitute’ (válaðr ‘poor, wretched’) — sense is the one wealth the poor can rely on.

sentences.t:
Byrði betri
berrat maðr brauto at,
en sé manvit mikit;
vegnest verra
vegra hann velli at,
en sé ofdrykkia ǫls.
sentences.l:
A better burden
a man bears not on the road
than much good sense;
a worse provision
he carries not over the field
than too much ale-drinking.
sentences.i: No better load can a man carry on his travels than plenty of common sense; and no worse road-provision than drinking too much ale.
sentences.n: The first half repeats stanza 10 word for word — a deliberate ‘tvískelfing’ (twinned opening) pivoting to a new point. vegnest ‘journey-provisions’ (way-food). ofdrykkia ǫls ‘over-drinking of ale’ (ǫls, gen. of ǫl) — the theme of the next stanzas.

sentences.t:
Era svá gott,
sem gott kveða,
ǫl alda sonom;
þvíat færa veit,
er fleira drekkr,
síns til geðs gumi.
sentences.i: Ale is not the good thing for men that it is made out to be; for the more a man drinks, the less he keeps of his own wits.
sentences.n: Era = er-a ‘is not’. alda sonom ‘for the sons of men’ (poetic ‘for men’). The last three lines are compressed: ‘the less (a man) knows of his own mind (geð), the more he drinks’ — drink and self-command run opposite. færa … fleira: comparatives ‘the less … the more’.

sentences.t:
Óminnis hegri heitir,
sá er yfir ǫlðrom þrumir:
hann stelr geði guma;
þess fugls fiǫðrom
ek fiǫtraðr vark
í garði Gunnlaðar.
sentences.l:
The heron of forgetfulness it is called,
the one that broods over ale-feasts:
it steals a man's mind;
with that bird's feathers
I was fettered
in Gunnlǫð's court.
sentences.i: There is a bird men call the heron of oblivion, that hovers over the drinking; it steals a man's wits away. By its feathers I too was shackled, in Gunnlǫð's dwelling.
sentences.n: Óminnis hegri ‘heron of forgetfulness’ — a striking kenning for drunkenness, imagined as a bird brooding over the ǫlðr (ale-feast). The speaker is now Odin: Gunnlǫð is the giantess who guarded the mead of poetry, which Odin won (see the Rúnatal later). ek … vark ‘I was’ (vark, old 1st sg. past of vera). fiǫtraðr ‘fettered’.

sentences.t:
Ǫlr ek varð,
varð ofrǫlvi
at ins fróða Fialars;
því er ǫlðr batst,
at aptr of heimtir
hverr sitt geð gumi.
sentences.l:
Drunk I became,
I became dead-drunk
at wise Fialarr's;
that is the best of ale-feasts,
when each man gets back
his own mind afterward.
sentences.i: Drunk I grew, blind drunk, at wise Fialar's; but the best of any ale-feast is the one a man comes away from with his wits still his own.
sentences.n: Odin's confession continues. ofrǫlvi ‘over-ale-ish, dead drunk’ (of- intensive + ǫlvi from ǫlr ‘drunk’). Fialarr: a name linked to the mead-of-poetry story. ǫlðr batst ‘the best (of) ale-feast’ (batst = superlative of vel/góðr). aptr of heimtir … sitt geð: ‘gets back his own mind’ — the moral turn after the boast.

sentences.t:
Þagalt ok hugalt
skyli þióðans barn
ok vígdiarft vera;
glaðr ok reifr
skyli gumna hverr,
unz sinn bíðr bana.
sentences.l:
Reserved and thoughtful
should a prince's child be
and bold in war;
glad and cheerful
should every man be,
until he awaits his death.
sentences.i: A king's son should be discreet, thoughtful, and brave in battle; and every man should be glad and cheerful until the day he meets his death.
sentences.n: Dagalt: ‘reserved, sparing of speech’ (a rare word; the sense ‘silent/discreet’). vígdiarft ‘war-bold’. þióðans barn ‘a prince's child’ (þióðann ‘king, lord’). unz sinn bíðr bana ‘until he awaits his death’ — face life cheerfully to the end.

sentences.t:
Ósniallr maðr
hyggz muno ey lifa,
ef hann við víg varaz;
en elli gefr
hánom engi frið,
þótt hánom geirar gefi.
sentences.l:
The cowardly man
thinks he will live forever,
if he keeps away from war;
but old age gives him
no peace,
though spears may spare him.
sentences.i: The coward imagines he will live forever if only he shuns battle — but old age grants him no truce, even where spears let him be.
sentences.n: Ósniallr ‘un-valiant, cowardly’. hyggz … muno ey lifa ‘thinks he will live forever’ (ey ‘ever’). The point cuts against caution taken too far: you cannot dodge death by dodging danger, for elli ‘old age’ comes regardless.

sentences.t:
Kópir afglapi,
er til kynnis kømr,
þylsk hann um eða þrumir;
allt er senn,
ef hann sylg um getr:
uppi er þá geð guma.
sentences.i: The fool gawps when he comes as a guest, muttering under his breath or sitting mute; but the moment he gets a drink, out it all comes — his whole mind is on show.
sentences.n: Kópir ‘gapes, stares stupidly’; afglapi ‘fool, dolt’. þylsk … um ‘mumbles to himself’ (middle of þylja); þrumir ‘sits torpid, broods’. uppi er … geð ‘the mind is up/out, exposed’ — drink undoes the fool who cannot hold his tongue.

sentences.t:
Sá einn veit,
er víða ratar
ok hefir fiǫlð um farit,
hverio geði
stýrir gumna hverr,
sá er vitandi er vits.
sentences.l:
He alone knows,
who travels widely
and has fared far and wide,
by what disposition
every man is governed —
he who has understanding of his wits.
sentences.i: Only the man who has travelled widely and seen much truly knows what temper rules each person he meets — the man, that is, who has his wits about him.
sentences.n: A counterweight to stanza 5: wide travel, joined to sense, teaches how to read people. hverio geði … stýrir ‘by what disposition (each) is steered’. sá er vitandi er vits ‘he who is knowing of wit’ — travel only teaches the already-shrewd.

sentences.t:
Haldit maðr á keri,
drekki þó at hófi miǫð,
mæli þarft eða þegi;
ókynnis þess
vár þik engi maðr,
at þu gangir snemma at sofa.
sentences.l:
Let a man not cling to the cup,
yet let him drink mead in measure,
let him speak what is needful or be silent;
of discourtesy in this
no man will blame you,
that you go early to sleep.
sentences.i: Don't hog the cup — but drink your share of the mead; say what needs saying or hold your peace. No one will fault you for bad manners if you go to bed early.
sentences.n: Haldit = haldi-at ‘let (him) not hold on’. at hófi ‘in moderation’ (hóf ‘measure’). mæli þarft eða þegi ‘let him speak what is useful or be silent’. vár … engi maðr ‘no man will reproach/accuse’ (vá ‘to blame’); ókynnis (gen.) ‘for discourtesy’. Leaving early sober is no rudeness.

sentences.t:
Gráðugr halr,
nema geðs viti,
etr sér aldrtrega;
opt fær hlœgis,
er með horskom kømr,
manni heimskom magi.
sentences.l:
The greedy man,
unless he minds his sense,
eats himself into lifelong grief;
often his belly
brings a wise man's laughter
upon the foolish man, when he comes among the shrewd.
sentences.i: The greedy man, if he doesn't watch himself, eats his way into lasting misery; among sensible folk, a fool's belly often makes him a laughing-stock.
sentences.n: Gráðugr ‘greedy’; halr ‘man’. aldrtrega ‘lifelong grief’ (aldr ‘life’ + tregi ‘grief’). fær hlœgis ‘gets (cause for) laughter’, i.e. earns ridicule; magi ‘belly, stomach’ is the subject that shames the heimskr (foolish) man among the horskir (wise). Gluttony, like drink, exposes lack of self-command.

glossary.alda: ǫld (f.) — age; (pl.) men, people; here gen. pl., 'of men' (alda sonom 'for the sons of men')
glossary.aldrtrega: aldrtregi (m.) — lifelong grief (aldr 'life' + tregi 'grief'); here acc. sg.
glossary.allar: allr — all; here nom./acc. pl. fem.
glossary.allt: allr — all, everything; here nom./acc. sg. neut.
glossary.annars: annarr — other, another; here gen. sg., 'of another' (annars brióstom 'in another's breast')
glossary.at: at — that (conj.); to (before an infinitive); at (prep., 'at, with'); also the suffixed negative -at on verbs
glossary.augabragði: augabragð (n.) — the twinkling of an eye; a mockery; at augabragði verðr 'becomes a laughing-stock' (dat. sg.)
glossary.augom: auga (n.) — eye; here dat. pl., 'with the eyes'
glossary.auði: auðr (m.) — wealth, riches; here dat. sg., 'than wealth' (auði betra)
glossary.bana: bani (m.) — death; slayer; here acc. sg., 'death' (bíðr bana 'awaits his death')
glossary.batst: beztr / batst — best (superlative, neut.); ǫlðr batst 'the best of ale-feasts'
glossary.berrat: bera — to bear, carry; berrat = berr + -at, '(he) bears not' (3rd sg. pres.)
glossary.betra: betri — better; here neut., 'better' (betra en 'better than')
glossary.brauto: braut (f.) — road, way; here dat. sg. (brauto at 'on the road')
glossary.brióstom: brióst (n.) — breast; here dat. pl. (annars brióstom í/ór 'in/out of another's breast')
glossary.brǫndom: brandr (m.) — firebrand, burning log; here dat. pl., 'at the fire-logs' (the hearth)
glossary.bíðr: bíða — to wait for, await (+ gen.); here 3rd sg. pres., 'awaits'
glossary.drekki: drekka — to drink; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'let him drink'
glossary.drekkr: drekka — to drink; here 3rd sg. pres., 'drinks'
glossary.dælt: dæll — easy, undemanding; here neut., 'easy' (dælt er heima hvat 'everything is easy at home')
glossary.einn: einn — one; alone; sá einn 'he alone'
glossary.ekki: ekki — nothing; not at all (neut. of engi)
glossary.elz: eldr (m.) — fire; here gen. sg. (elz er þǫrf 'there is need of fire')
glossary.endrþǫgo: endrþǫgn (f.) — silence in return, answering silence (endr 'again, in return' + þǫgn 'silence'); here gen. sg.
glossary.er: vera — to be (3rd sg. pres. 'is'); also the relative particle 'who, which, that'
glossary.etr: eta — to eat; here 3rd sg. pres., 'eats' (etr sér 'eats for himself')
glossary.eyrom: eyra (n.) — ear; here dat. pl., 'with the ears'
glossary.farit: fara — to go, travel; here past participle, 'travelled' (um fiall farit)
glossary.fialars: Fialarr (m.) — Fialar, a proper name (here gen. sg.); a figure linked to the mead of poetry
glossary.fiall: fiall (n.) — mountain, fell; here acc. sg. (um fiall farit 'travelled over the mountain')
glossary.fiǫlð: fiǫlð (f.) — a great deal, multitude; here acc., 'far and wide, much' (fiǫlð um farit)
glossary.fiǫtraðr: fiǫtra — to fetter, shackle; here past participle masc., 'fettered'
glossary.fiǫðrom: fiǫðr (f.) — feather; here dat. pl., 'with the feathers'
glossary.fleira: fleiri — more; here neut. comparative, 'the more'
glossary.fleti: flet (n.) — the raised plank-floor / benches of a hall; here dat. sg. (á fleti fyrir 'on the benches ahead')
glossary.frama: frami (m.) — advancement, distinction, worth; here gen. sg. (freista frama 'to test one's worth')
glossary.freista: freista (+ gen.) — to try, test, make trial of (infinitive)
glossary.frið: friðr (m.) — peace; here acc. sg. (engi frið 'no peace')
glossary.fróða: fróðr — wise, learned; here weak masc., 'the wise' (ins fróða Fialars 'of wise Fialar')
glossary.fróðra: fróðr — wise; here gen. pl. (fróðra hverr 'every wise man')
glossary.fugls: fugl (m.) — bird; here gen. sg. (þess fugls 'of that bird')
glossary.fyrir: fyrir — before, ahead, in front (adv./prep.); nýsiz fyrir 'scouts ahead'
glossary.fær: fá — to get, obtain; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gets'
glossary.færa: fár / færi — fewer, less; here neut. comparative, 'the less'
glossary.gangi: ganga — to go, walk; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, '(before) one goes'
glossary.gangir: ganga — to go; here 2nd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'that you go'
glossary.garði: garðr (m.) — enclosure, court, dwelling; here dat. sg. (í garði Gunnlaðar 'in Gunnlǫð's court')
glossary.gefendr: gefandi (m.) — giver; here nom. pl., 'the givers' (of hospitality), i.e. the hosts (pres. part. of gefa)
glossary.gefi: gefa — to give; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'may give' (þótt geirar gefi)
glossary.gefr: gefa — to give; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gives'
glossary.geta: geta — to get; here infinitive, 'to get' (gott geta 'to get benefit')
glossary.getr: geta — to get; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gets' (sér um getr 'gets for himself')
glossary.geði: geð (n.) — mind; here dat. sg. (at geði 'in mind'; hverio geði 'by what disposition')
glossary.geðs: geð (n.) — mind; here gen. sg. (geðs viti 'minds his sense'; til geðs 'of his mind')
glossary.gott: góðr — good; here neut., 'good' (gott geta 'to get good/benefit')
glossary.guma: gumi (m.) — man; here gen./acc. sg. (geð guma 'a man's mind')
glossary.gumna: gumi (m.) — man; here gen. pl. (gumna hverr 'every man')
glossary.gunnlaðar: Gunnlǫð (f.) — Gunnlǫð, the giantess who guarded the mead of poetry; here gen. sg.
glossary.gáttir: gátt (f.) — doorway, door-opening; here acc. pl., 'the doorways'
glossary.gætinn: gætinn — heedful, wary, cautious (nom. sg. masc.)
glossary.góðs: góðr — good; here gen. sg. neut. (góðs um œðis 'of a good disposition')
glossary.haldit: halda — to hold; haldit = haldi + -at, 'let (him) not hold on' (subjunctive + neg.); halda á keri 'cling to the cup'
glossary.hefir: hafa — to have; here 3rd sg. pres., 'has'
glossary.hegri: hegri (m.) — heron; Óminnis hegri 'the heron of forgetfulness' (nom. sg.)
glossary.heilir: heill — hale, whole, sound; as a greeting 'hail!'; here nom. pl. masc.
glossary.heimisgarða: heimisgarðr (m.) — homestead, home-enclosure; here acc. pl. (kømr heimisgarða til 'comes to a homestead')
glossary.heimskom: heimskr — foolish; here dat. sg. masc. (manni heimskom 'to the foolish man')
glossary.heimtir: heimta — to draw home, get back, reclaim; here 3rd sg. pres. (aptr of heimtir 'gets back')
glossary.heitir: heita — to be called; here 3rd sg. pres., 'is called'
glossary.hinn: hinn — that one; hinn er sæll 'he is fortunate, who…' (demonstrative)
glossary.hlióði: hlióð (n.) — hearing; silence; here dat. sg. (þunno hlióði þegir 'is silent with taut hearing')
glossary.hlýðir: hlýða — to listen; here 3rd sg. pres., 'listens'
glossary.hlœgis: hlœgi (n.) — laughter, ridicule; here gen. sg. (fær hlœgis 'earns ridicule')
glossary.horskom: horskr — wise, shrewd; here dat. pl. (með horskom 'among the wise')
glossary.hvat: hvat — what; anything (heima hvat 'anything at home')
glossary.hverio: hverr — each, every; here dat. sg. neut. (hverio geði 'by what/each disposition')
glossary.hverr: hverr — each, every; every one (nom. sg. masc.)
glossary.hyggiandi: hyggiandi (f.) — thought, understanding, judgement; here dat. sg. (at hyggiandi sinni 'of his understanding')
glossary.hyggz: hyggia — to think; here 3rd sg. pres. middle, 'thinks (to himself)' (hyggz muno lifa 'thinks he will live')
glossary.hánom: hann — he; here dat. sg., 'to him, for him'
glossary.hófi: hóf (n.) — moderation, measure; here dat. sg. (at hófi 'in moderation')
glossary.ill: illr — bad, ill; here nom. pl. neut. (ill ráð 'bad counsel')
glossary.inn: inn — in, inward (adv. of motion); also the article inn 'the' (inn vari gestr 'the wary guest')
glossary.ins: inn (article) — the; here gen. sg. masc. (ins fróða Fialars 'of wise Fialar')
glossary.kalinn: kala — to chill, freeze; here past participle masc., 'chilled, numb' (á kné kalinn 'frozen to the knee')
glossary.kann: kunna — to know, be able; here 3rd sg. pres., 'knows'
glossary.keri: ker (n.) — cup, vessel; here dat. sg. (halda á keri 'hold the cup')
glossary.kné: kné (n.) — knee; here acc. sg. (á kné kalinn 'chilled to the knee')
glossary.kominn: koma — to come; here past participle masc., 'come, arrived' (er kominn 'has come')
glossary.kveða: kveða — to say, declare; here 3rd pl. pres., 'they say' (sem gott kveða 'as it is called good')
glossary.kynnis: kynni (n.) — acquaintance, a visit; here gen. sg. (til kynnis kømr 'comes visiting')
glossary.kópir: kópa — to gape, stare stupidly; here 3rd sg. pres., 'gapes'
glossary.kømr: koma — to come; here 3rd sg. pres., 'comes' (Neckel's spelling with ø; later Icelandic kemr)
glossary.lifir: lifa — to live; here 3rd sg. pres., 'lives' (meðan lifir 'while he lives')
glossary.líknstafi: líknstafr (m.) — a token/word of favour, kindly regard; here acc. pl.
glossary.manni: maðr (m.) — man; here dat. sg., 'to/for a man'
glossary.matar: matr (m.) — food; here gen. sg. (matar þǫrf 'need of food')
glossary.mikit: mikill — great, much; here neut., 'much' (manvit mikit 'much good sense')
glossary.miǫk: miǫk — much, very, greatly (adv.); Neckel's spelling with ǫ (later Icelandic mjök)
glossary.miǫð: miǫðr (m.) — mead; here acc. sg. (drekki miǫð 'drink mead')
glossary.muno: munu — shall, will (auxiliary); here infinitive, 'will' (muno ey lifa 'will live forever')
glossary.mæli: mæla — to speak; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'let him speak'
glossary.mætti: mega — to be able, may; here 3rd sg. past subjunctive, 'could' (ef sér geta mætti 'if he could get')
glossary.nýsiz: nýsa — to peer, investigate; here 3rd sg. pres. middle, 'feels his way, scouts' (nýsiz fyrir)
glossary.of: of — an old expletive particle before verbs (of heimtir); also 'over, about'
glossary.ofdrykkia: ofdrykkia (f.) — over-drinking, excess in drink; here nom. sg. (ofdrykkia ǫls 'over-drinking of ale')
glossary.ofrǫlvi: ofrǫlvi — dead-drunk, over-drunk (of- intensive + ǫlvi 'drunk'); here nom. sg. masc.
glossary.orz: orð (n.) — word, speech; here gen. sg. (orz ok endrþǫgo 'of speech and silence-in-return')
glossary.ratar: rata — to travel, wander, roam; here 3rd sg. pres., 'travels' (víða ratar 'travels widely')
glossary.ráð: ráð (n.) — counsel, advice; here nom./acc. pl. (ill ráð 'bad counsel')
glossary.senn: senn — at once, all at once
glossary.sinn: sinn — his own (reflexive possessive); here acc. sg. (sinn bana 'his own death')
glossary.sinni: sinn — his own; here dat. sg. fem. (at hyggiandi sinni 'of his understanding')
glossary.sitia: sitja — to sit (infinitive); Neckel's spelling with i for j
glossary.sitr: sitja — to sit; here 3rd sg. pres., 'sits'
glossary.sitt: sinn — his own; here nom./acc. sg. neut. (sitt geð 'his own mind')
glossary.siá: siá — this, this one (demonstrative pron., nom. sg. masc.); Neckel's spelling of sjá
glossary.siálfr: siálfr — self, himself; here nom. sg. masc. (siálfr um á 'himself has')
glossary.skal: skulu — shall, must; here 3rd sg. pres., 'shall'
glossary.skoðar: skoða — to look at, examine; here 3rd sg. pres., 'looks about'
glossary.skoðaz: skoða — to look over; here with -z middle, 'be scanned' (um skoðaz skyli 'should be scanned')
glossary.skygnaz: skygnaz — to look about, peer around (a -z/-sk middle verb); here pres. subjunctive
glossary.skyli: skulu — shall, ought; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'should' (the gnomic 'one should')
glossary.slíkt: slíkr — such; here neut., 'such (is)' (slíkt er 'such is')
glossary.snotrom: snotr — wise, sensible; here dat. pl. (með snotrom 'among the wise')
glossary.sonom: sonr (m.) — son; here dat. pl. (alda sonom 'for the sons of men')
glossary.stað: staðr (m.) — place; here dat. sg. (í ókunnom stað 'in an unknown place')
glossary.stelr: stela (+ dat.) — to steal; here 3rd sg. pres., 'steals' (stelr geði 'steals the mind')
glossary.stýrir: stýra (+ dat.) — to steer, govern; here 3rd sg. pres., 'governs'
glossary.sylg: sylgr (m.) — a drink, draught; here acc. sg. (sylg um getr 'gets a drink')
glossary.sá: sá — that, that one; he (demonstrative pron., nom. sg. masc.); sá er 'he who'
glossary.sæll: sæll — fortunate, happy, blessed (nom. sg. masc.)
glossary.sé: vera — to be; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'be, may be' (þótt sé; en sé)
glossary.sér: sik — oneself; here dat. reflexive, 'for himself, to himself'
glossary.síns: sinn — his own; here gen. sg. (síns frama 'of his worth'; síns til geðs 'of his mind')
glossary.til: til — to, toward; (with gen.) of; til kynnis 'to a visit'; til geðs 'of his mind'
glossary.um: um — an old expletive particle before verbs (um getr, um á, um farit); also 'about, over, around'
glossary.uppi: uppi — up, out, exposed (adv.); uppi er geð 'the mind is laid bare'
glossary.varaz: varask (+ acc.) — to beware, guard against; here 3rd sg. pres. middle (við víg varaz 'guards against war')
glossary.vari: varr — wary, cautious; here weak masc., 'the wary' (inn vari gestr)
glossary.vark: vera — to be; here old 1st sg. past, 'I was' (ek fiǫtraðr vark)
glossary.varð: verða — to become; here 1st/3rd sg. past, 'became' (ǫlr ek varð 'drunk I became')
glossary.vatz: vatn (n.) — water; here gen. sg. (vatz er þǫrf 'there is need of water')
glossary.vegnest: vegnest (n.) — journey-provisions, road-food (vegr 'way' + nest 'provisions'); here nom./acc. sg.
glossary.vegra: vega — to carry, bear (a load); vegra = vegr + suffixed negative -a, '(he) carries not'
glossary.veit: vita — to know; here 3rd sg. pres., 'knows'
glossary.velli: vǫllr (m.) — field, open ground; here dat. sg. (velli at 'on the field')
glossary.verra: verri — worse; here neut. comparative (vegnest verra 'a worse provision')
glossary.verðar: verðr (m.) — meal; here gen. sg. (til verðar kømr 'comes to a meal')
glossary.verðr: verða — to become; here 3rd sg. pres., 'becomes' (at augabragði verðr); also 'befalls' (víti verðr)
glossary.vin: vinr (m.) — friend; here acc. sg. (óbrigðra vin 'a more faithful friend')
glossary.vitandi: vita — to know; here pres. participle, 'knowing' (vitandi vits 'having understanding of wit')
glossary.viti: vita — to know; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'has/minds' (geðs viti 'minds his sense')
glossary.vits: vit (n.) — wit, sense; here gen. sg. (vitandi vits 'knowing of wit')
glossary.vitz: vit (n.) — wit; here gen. sg. (vitz er þǫrf 'there is need of wit'); Neckel spells vitz
glossary.við: við — against; with; toward (prep.); við víg varaz 'guards against war'; ódælla er við þat 'harder with that'
glossary.válaðs: válaðr (m.) — a destitute, wretched man; here gen. sg. (válaðs vera 'the refuge of the destitute')
glossary.vár: vá — to blame, find fault, reproach; here 3rd sg. pres. (vár þik engi maðr 'no man will reproach you')
glossary.váða: váð (f.) — cloth, clothing; here gen. sg. (matar ok váða 'of food and clothing')
glossary.víg: víg (n.) — battle, war, killing; here acc. sg. (við víg varaz 'guards against war')
glossary.vígdiarft: vígdiarfr — bold in war; here neut., 'war-bold'
glossary.víti: víti (n.) — harm, penalty, mishap; here nom./acc. sg. (víti verðr 'harm befalls')
glossary.vǫrom: varr — wary; here dat. pl. (víti vǫrom 'harm to the wary')
glossary.yfir: yfir — over, above (prep.); yfir ǫlðrom þrumir 'broods over ale-feasts'
glossary.á: á — on, upon, at (prep.); also 'has, owns' (3rd sg. of eiga: siálfr um á 'himself has')
glossary.óbrigðra: óbrigðr — unfailing, faithful; here comparative, 'a more faithful' (óbrigðra vin)
glossary.ódælla: ódæll — difficult, hard to deal with; here comparative neut., 'harder' (ódælla er við þat)
glossary.ókunnom: ókunnr — unknown, unfamiliar; here dat. sg. (í ókunnom stað 'in an unknown place')
glossary.ókynnis: ókynni (n.) — discourtesy, bad manners; here gen. sg. (ókynnis vár 'blame for discourtesy')
glossary.óminnis: óminni (n.) — forgetfulness, oblivion; here gen. sg. (Óminnis hegri 'heron of forgetfulness')
glossary.ór: ór — out of, from (prep.); annars brióstom ór 'out of another's breast'
glossary.óvinir: óvinr (m.) — enemy, foe (ó- 'un-' + vinr 'friend'); here nom. pl.
glossary.óvíst: óvíss — uncertain; here neut., 'uncertain' (óvíst er at vita 'it is uncertain to know')
glossary.þarft: þarfr — needful, useful; here neut., 'what is needful' (mæli þarft 'speak what is useful')
glossary.þagalt: þagall — silent, reserved, discreet; here neut., 'discreet' (Þagalt ok hugalt)
glossary.þegi: þegja — to be silent; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, 'let him be silent'
glossary.þegir: þegja — to be silent; here 3rd sg. pres., 'is silent'
glossary.þegit: þiggja — to receive, accept; here past participle, 'received' (opt þegit 'often received')
glossary.þeim: sá / þeir — that one; them; here dat., 'to/for him' or 'to them'
glossary.þeims: þeim es — 'for him who' (þeim + relative particle es/er)
glossary.þerro: þerra (f.) — a towel, drying-cloth; here gen. sg. (þerro ok þióðlaðar)
glossary.þess: sá — that; here gen. sg., 'of that' (þess fugls 'of that bird'; ókynnis þess 'of this discourtesy')
glossary.þikkir: þykkja — to seem; here 3rd sg. pres., 'seems' (auði betra þikkir 'seems better than wealth')
glossary.þióðans: þióðann (m.) — king, prince, lord; here gen. sg. (þióðans barn 'a prince's child')
glossary.þióðlaðar: þióðlǫð (f.) — a hearty invitation, warm welcome; here gen. sg.
glossary.þrumir: þruma — to sit torpid, brood, hover; here 3rd sg. pres. (yfir ǫlðrom þrumir 'broods over ale-feasts')
glossary.þu: þú — you (2nd sg.); Neckel's spelling þu (unaccented)
glossary.þunno: þunnr — thin; here dat. sg. neut. (þunno hlióði 'with taut/strained hearing')
glossary.því: því — for, because (því at); therefore; (instrumental of sá) 'by that'
glossary.þvíat: þvíat — because, for (Neckel writes it as one word)
glossary.þylsk: þylja — to mutter, recite under one's breath; here 3rd sg. pres. middle, 'mumbles to himself'
glossary.þǫrf: þǫrf (f.) — need; er þǫrf (+ gen.) 'there is need of' (nom. sg.)
glossary.œðis: œði (n.) — disposition, temper, manner; here gen. sg. (góðs um œðis 'of good manner')
glossary.ǫlr: ǫlr — drunk, tipsy (nom. sg. masc.); ǫlr ek varð 'drunk I became'
glossary.ǫls: ǫl (n.) — ale; here gen. sg. (ofdrykkia ǫls 'over-drinking of ale')
glossary.ǫlðr: ǫlðr (n.) — ale-feast, drinking-bout; here nom. sg. (ǫlðr batst 'the best of ale-feasts')
glossary.ǫlðrom: ǫlðr (n.) — ale-feast; here dat. pl. (yfir ǫlðrom 'over ale-feasts')
